title: Height Percentile Calculator – Height Ranking by Age & Gender
tagline: Calculate your height percentile based on age, gender, and height using CDC growth charts and statistical analysis.
intro: Enter your age, gender, and height to get an accurate height percentile ranking and growth category compared to the reference population.

error.invalidAge: Please enter a valid age (2–80 years).

about.heading: About the Height Percentile Calculator
about.body:
Height percentile is a statistical measure that describes where an individual's height falls relative to a reference population of the same age and sex. A percentile of 50 means exactly half the people in the reference group are shorter and half are taller. A percentile of 90 means the person is taller than 90% of their peers. Percentiles are widely used in medicine, sports science, nutrition research, and public health to assess growth, monitor development, and identify outliers who may require further evaluation.

This calculator uses height reference data derived from the CDC (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention) growth charts and the NHANES (National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey) database. The CDC provides sex-specific and age-specific height distributions for children aged 2–20 years, based on nationally representative samples. For adults, reference data from NHANES surveys is used to characterize the adult height distribution by sex.

The calculation method is based on the z-score approach. A z-score represents how many standard deviations a measurement is above or below the population mean. For children, the LMS (Lambda-Mu-Sigma) method is used to convert height to a z-score, accounting for the fact that height distributions are not perfectly symmetric at all ages. For adults, a simple z-score is computed using mean and standard deviation from the reference population. The z-score is then converted to a percentile using the standard normal cumulative distribution function.

For children and adolescents, the percentile takes on particular clinical significance. Pediatric endocrinologists and growth specialists use height percentile trajectories to identify growth disorders. A child consistently tracking at the 5th percentile who suddenly drops to the 2nd percentile warrants investigation. Conversely, a child who has always been short but tracks consistently along a percentile line may simply be expressing familial short stature. The current height percentile should always be interpreted in the context of parental heights (mid-parental height), growth velocity, bone age, and overall clinical assessment.

For adults, height percentile is primarily informational — adult height is determined and does not change substantially after growth plates close (typically in the late teens). However, adult height percentile is relevant in medical contexts such as evaluating height loss in older adults (which may indicate vertebral compression fractures), assessing height relative to peers in epidemiological research, and in sports and ergonomic applications.

Ethnicity influences height distributions. Asian populations, on average, are somewhat shorter than European or African populations, though there is enormous within-group variation and the gaps have been narrowing as nutrition and socioeconomic conditions improve globally. This calculator currently uses a unified reference population from US national surveys. Ethnicity is captured as optional context but does not alter the core calculation, which follows standard CDC reference norms.

examples.heading: Height Percentile Examples
examples.intro: These examples show height percentile calculations across different ages, genders, and heights using CDC reference data.

items.note: Above average 10-year-old boy. Normal growth pattern, consistent with healthy development.

howto.heading: How to Use the Height Percentile Calculator
steps: Enter your age in completed years (e.g., 14 for a 14-year-old, 30 for a 30-year-old adult).
Select your gender — height distributions are sex-specific, so this is required for an accurate percentile.
Enter your height and select the measurement unit (centimeters or inches).
Optionally select your ethnicity for additional context — note this does not change the CDC-based calculation.
Click Calculate to see your height percentile, z-score, and growth category.

faq.heading: Height Percentile Calculator FAQ
faq.q1: What height percentile is considered normal?
faq.q1_a: Percentiles between the 3rd and 97th are generally considered within the normal range for height. The 10th to 90th percentile range is often described as 'average' in clinical practice. However, a consistent tracking along any percentile line from childhood through adolescence is usually reassuring — the trajectory matters as much as the absolute value.
faq.q2: At what age does height stop increasing?
faq.q2_a: Most females stop growing by age 15–17, and most males by age 17–19, when the growth plates (epiphyseal plates) in the long bones fuse. The timing varies by individual and is influenced by genetics, nutrition, and hormonal factors. Some males may continue growing into their early twenties. After peak height is reached, minor natural height loss begins in the thirties and accelerates after age 50 due to spinal compression and postural changes.
faq.q3: My child is below the 5th percentile — should I be concerned?
faq.q3_a: A height below the 5th percentile warrants evaluation, especially if it represents a recent drop from a higher percentile, if the child's growth velocity has slowed, or if there are other symptoms. Causes include familial short stature (parents are also short), constitutional delay (late bloomer), or medical conditions such as growth hormone deficiency, hypothyroidism, celiac disease, or Turner syndrome in girls. A pediatric endocrinologist can perform a complete assessment.
faq.q4: How does ethnicity affect height percentiles?
faq.q4_a: Different ethnic groups have different average heights, partly due to genetics and partly due to environmental factors like nutrition and healthcare access. For example, Asian populations tend to have shorter average heights than European or African-descent populations in US surveys. The CDC reference charts used in this calculator are based on the diverse US population and are considered broadly applicable, though specialized ethnic-specific charts exist for some populations in research contexts.
faq.q5: Can I use this calculator for adults?
faq.q5_a: Yes. For adults aged 20 and older, the calculator uses reference data from US national health surveys (NHANES) to compute height percentile relative to the adult population of the same sex. Adult height percentile is stable (since height doesn't change substantially after skeletal maturity) and is useful for contextual understanding, ergonomic design, sports scouting, and epidemiological comparisons.
faq.q6: What is the difference between height percentile and height z-score?
faq.q6_a: Both describe the same thing in different formats. The z-score tells you how many standard deviations from the population mean a measurement falls: a z-score of 0 is exactly average, +1 is one SD above average, −2 is two SDs below average. The percentile converts the z-score to a more intuitive format: z = 0 corresponds to the 50th percentile, z = +1.65 to the 95th percentile, z = −2.0 to the 2.3rd percentile. Clinicians often use z-scores for statistical analysis and percentiles for patient communication.
